steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an Inferences question. The passage gives a critical threshold: a handedness study needs at least 176 individuals for a representative result. Both Chen's study and Brooks's study used only 8 individuals — far below that threshold. The correct choice must follow logically from this shared limitation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the study by Chen reliably represents handedness in captive primates but not in wild primates.: Treats Chen's study (8 individuals) as reliable. But 8 is far below the 176-individual minimum, so neither study can be considered reliable. ✗
  • Choice B — Brooks likely underestimated the prevalence of right-handedness among the wild chimpanzees in the study.: Assumes Brooks's study is large enough to draw directional conclusions about underestimating. With only 8 subjects, no such inference is supported. ✗
  • Choice C — neither the study by Chen nor the study by Brooks provides sufficient evidence to make a meaningful comparison about handedness in primates.: Applies the 176-individual threshold to both studies equally — both had 8 individuals, so neither can provide sufficient evidence. This follows directly from the passage's logic. ✓
  • Choice D — the study by Chen reliably represents handedness in captive primates, but the study by Brooks likely does not reliably represent handedness in wild primates.: Again treats Chen's study as reliable despite its 8-individual sample. The threshold applies to both studies the same way. ✗
That is the correct answer.
